lines: On average, U.S. inhabitants receive a radiation dose of about 3.1 mSv each year. This dose
comes from natural background radiation. Most of this background exposure comes from
radon in the air, with smaller amounts from cosmic rays and the Earth itself. An additional
2.2 mSv comes from man-made sources of radiation (mostly exposure from medical
procedures, and some commercial and industrial sources). In general, a yearly dose of 5.3
mSv from all radiation sources has not been shown to cause humans any harm. Figure 2
breaks down sources of background radiation as discussed in NCRP report #184. Figure 3
shows specific examples of common radiation exposures.

lines: Lethal dose (LD). The dose of radiation expected to cause death to 50 percent of an exposed
population within a period of time (e.g. 30 days (LD 50/30)). Typically, the LD 50/30 is in
the range from 400 to 450 rem (4 to 5 sieverts) received over a very short period.

lines: Each sealed source must be tested at intervals not to exceed 6 months. In the absence of a
certificate from a transferor that a test has been made within the 6 months before the transfer,
the sealed source may not be used until tested. If a sealed source has leaked, a licensee shall
file a report within 5 days if a required leak test reveals the presence of 185 Bq (0.005 μCi) or
more of removable contamination. The report must be filed with the appropriate NRC
Regional Office with a copy to the Director, Office of Nuclear Material Safety and
Safeguards. The written report must include the model number and serial number, if assigned,
of the leaking source, the radionuclide and its estimated activity, the results of the test, the
date of the test, and the corrective action taken.

lines: Radio-thin-layer Chromatography (radio-TLC) is commonly used to analyze purity of
radiopharmaceuticals or to determine the reaction conversion when optimizing radiosynthesis
processes. In applications where there are few radioactive species, radio-TLC is preferred
over radio-high-performance liquid chromatography due to its simplicity and relatively quick
analysis time. Most approaches to radio-TLC were merely extensions or modifications of
techniques that had been used in paper chromatography (PC).

[p005] Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide
[p005] Introduction
[p005] Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) is an integral part of the domain of diagnostic radiology
[p005] and interventional radiology. Questions about RISC are included on the Qualifying (Core)
[p005] Exam (25 questions) and in the essentials module on the Certifying Exam (10 questions).
[p005] RISC questions count toward the overall exam score and are not scored separately.
[p005] These questions assess the candidate’s understanding and knowledge related to the safe use
[p005] of radioactive materials in the clinical practice of diagnostic or interventional radiology. The
[p005] domain encompasses but is not limited to the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)
[p005] requirements found in Part 35 of the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR): Medical Use of
[p005] Byproduct Material. The NRC defines byproduct material as radioactive material yielded in
[p005] or made radioactive by exposure to the radiation incident to the process of producing or using
[p005] special nuclear material. The RISC elements on the Certifying Exam are devoted to clinically
[p005] oriented practice scenarios, which emphasize evaluation of practical knowledge of
[p005] radioisotope safety and handling, as well as regulatory compliance.
[p005] A short practice domain document on the ABR website lists the domain critical concepts and
[p005] scope and provides an overview of the blueprint with estimated percentages of specific
[p005] content. Much of the information necessary to prepare for the exams, however, is spread
[p005] across multiple CFR reports and NRC documents. Reviewing this content can be a daunting
[p005] task for candidates preparing for the ABR Qualifying and Certifying exams. Given the scope
[p005] of expected knowledge and varied classroom/laboratory training and experience in radiology
[p005] departments, an in-depth study guide for candidates that would supplement their
[p005] NRC/authorized user (AU) training and experience during residency was identified as a
[p005] needed document.
[p005] All RISC questions on diagnostic radiology and interventional radiology exams will be based
[p005] on information that is covered in this document. This document will be maintained on an
[p005] ongoing basis to ensure the information continues to reflect current practice models and
[p005] regulatory requirements.
[p005] Please note: Some aspects of radiation safety and biology on the diagnostic radiology and
[p005] interventional radiology exams are from the physics section and are not covered in this
[p005] document.
[p005] 1. Radiation protection
[p005] 1.1 ALARA program
[p005] The annual average effective dose to the United States (U.S.) population from all sources of
[p005] radiation including background is 500 to 600 mrem (5 to 6 mSv).
[p005] The ALARA acronym stands for “as low as reasonably achievable,” referring to the judicious
[p005] use of nuclear radiology imaging such that the level of radiation exposure to patients and
[p005] personnel is as low as possible to yield a diagnosis. The Nuclear Regulatory Commission
[p005] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 5
[p006] (NRC) mandates annual review of the ALARA program by the Radiation Safety Officer
[p006] 1.1.1 Radiation protection program
[p006] Licensees must ensure that the highest dose to the public shall not exceed 100 mrem/year (1
[p006] mSv/yr). This dose also applies to hospital staff working outside of a radiology/imaging
[p006] department including but not limited to administration, support, nursing, engineering, or
[p006] transport personnel.
[p006] Time, shielding, and distance are the three main measures to decrease radiation exposure to
[p006] personnel.
[p006] Training and experience of authorized users, inventory of sealed sources and leak testing, and
[p006] a record of administered activity for diagnostic and therapeutic procedures are required in a
[p006] radiation protection program.
[p006] All personnel dosimeters (except for direct and indirect reading pocket ionization chambers
[p006] and those dosimeters used to measure the dose to the extremities) to determine the radiation
[p006] dose and that are used by licensees must be processed and evaluated by a dosimetry
[p006] processor. Personnel dosimetry records should be reviewed quarterly by the RSO. The
[p006] Radiation Safety Committee (RSC) must review dosimetry records every 3 months.
[p006] Individual dosimeters are required if personnel exposures are expected to exceed greater than
[p006] 10% of the annual occupational dose in a year.
[p006] 1.1.2 Audit program
[p006] Periodic, planned audits must be carried out at least every 12 months to verify compliance
[p006] with all aspects of the radiation protection program. These audits must be performed by
[p006] trained personnel not having direct responsibilities in the department/facility being audited.
[p006] Results from audits, including follow-up actions, must be documented and reviewed by
[p006] hospital/facility administration.
[p006] 1.2 Radiation areas
[p006] In nuclear radiology and radiation oncology departments, there are restricted and unrestricted
[p006] areas depending on the exposure rates and/or the presence of radioactive materials.
[p006] 1.2.1. Restricted areas
[p006] Examples of activities in radiation restricted areas include radiopharmaceutical preparation,
[p006] radiopharmaceutical administration, radiopharmaceutical storage, and imaging.
[p006] Signage required for various areas depending on radiation levels is shown in Figure 1.

[p007] 1.2.2. Public area
[p007] Unrestricted areas include reception, waiting areas, office spaces, and reading rooms. These
[p007] areas must have dose rates less than 2 mrem in any 1 hour (20 μSv/h) or less than 100

[p008] mrem/yr (1 mSv/yr), excluding dose contributions from patients administered radioactive
[p008] material and from patients being released.
[p008] Rooms or other areas in hospitals/facilities that are occupied by patients with administered
[p008] radioactive material are not required to be posted with caution signs provided that the patients
[p008] meet criteria for release (see section 6.5.1.3).
[p008] A room or area is not required to be posted with a caution sign in the presence of a sealed
[p008] source if the radiation level at 30 cm from the source surface container or housing does not
[p008] exceed 5 mrem (0.05 mSv) per hour.
[p008] 1.2.3. Caution signs
[p008] The standard radiation symbol authorized by the NRC uses the colors magenta, purple, or
[p008] black on a yellow background. Licensees are authorized to label sources, source holders, or
[p008] other device components without a color requirement. Refer to 1.2.1 for examples of caution
[p008] signage.
[p008] 2 Radiation biology
[p008] There are four different but interrelated ways to measure radiation: Radioactivity, Exposure,
[p008] Absorbed dose, and Dose equivalent. These can be remembered by the mnemonic R-E-A-D.
[p008] 2.1. Radioactivity
[p008] Radioactivity is a process by which an unstable atomic nucleus transitions to a stable
[p008] configuration by emitting radiation. The emitted radiation can be in the form of alpha or beta
[p008] particles, gamma rays, x-rays, or neutrons. A quantity of radioactive material is expressed in
[p008] terms of its radioactivity (or simply its activity), which represents how many atoms in the
[p008] material decay in a given time period. The units of measure for radioactivity are the curie (Ci)
[p008] or becquerel (Bq); note: 1 mCi = 37 MBq.
[p008] • Alpha particles are positively charged particles consisting of two protons and two
[p008] neutrons (a helium nucleus). They do not travel far in tissue (on average only 10-100
[p008] μm) and have a high linear energy transfer (LET), causing substantial
[p008] radiobiological effect to surrounding tissue if ingested, inhaled, or used for medical
[p008] therapeutic purposes.
[p008] • Beta particles are electrons (beta-minus) or positrons (beta-plus) emitted from a
[p008] decaying nucleus. Beta particles have greater penetration ability than alpha particles
[p008] (e.g., 131I beta-minus [negatron] travels 0.5-3.0 mm) but can be stopped by a few
[p008] millimeters of plastic. Beta-minus particles (electrons) have a moderate LET and can
[p008] be used for medical therapeutic purposes. Beta-plus particles (positrons) are highly
[p008] reactive and only travel a very short distance before combining in an annihilation
[p008] event with a beta-minus particle. The resulting annihilation event ejects two 511 keV
[p008] gamma rays in opposite directions. Annihilation photons are the foundation for PET
[p008] imaging.
[p008] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 8
[p009] • Gamma rays are electromagnetic waves (i.e., photons that do not have any mass)
[p009] emitted from the nucleus. Gamma rays have low LET and high penetration ability
[p009] and can pass through several centimeters of lead and are principally used for
[p009] imaging in general nuclear radiology studies.
[p009] • X-rays are electromagnetic waves (i.e., photons that do not have any mass) created
[p009] by beta-minus particles that interact with atomic nuclei and undergo Bremsstrahlung
[p009] radiation. As a beta-minus particle passes a high atomic mass nucleus (e.g., lead or
[p009] tungsten), its path is deflected, resulting in low energy x-ray emission.
[p009] • Neutron radiation is free neutrons emitted from the breaking apart of an atomic
[p009] nucleus. Neutron radiation can penetrate most materials and can cause nuclear
[p009] reactions in other materials (called chain reactions).
[p009] 2.1.1 Half-life
[p009] In determining radiation risk, it is important to understand the concept of half-life. The
[p009] different concepts of half-life that are important to remember are:
[p009] - The physical half-life (Tp) of a radionuclide is defined as the time it takes for 50% of
[p009] the radionuclide nuclei to decay.
[p009] - The biological half-life (Tb) of a radionuclide or radiopharmaceutical is defined as
[p009] the time it takes for the radionuclide or radiopharmaceutical concentration within the
[p009] body to reduce to 50% of the original concentration.
[p009] - The effective half-life (Te) of a radionuclide or radiopharmaceutical takes into
[p009] account the physical and biological half-life values and represents the combined
[p009] effects of physical nuclear decay and biological excretion.
[p009] 2.2 Radiation dose
[p009] Exposure describes the amount of radiation present in the air. The units for exposure are the
[p009] Roentgen (R) or coulomb/kilogram (C/kg). Typical measuring instruments are Geiger-
[p009] Mueller (GM) counters or ionization chambers and are measured in milliRoentgen (mR). The
[p009] exposure equivalent to one chest radiograph is ~10 mR.
[p009] 2.2.1 Absorbed dose
[p009] Absorbed dose describes the amount of radiation absorbed by tissue (that is, the amount of
[p009] energy that is deposited while passing through the tissue). Absorbed dose is scaled by the
[p009] mass of the tissue; thus, for the same amount of absorbed radiation, a smaller organ (e.g.,
[p009] kidneys) will have a greater absorbed dose value than a larger organ (e.g., liver). The units for
[p009] radiation absorbed dose are Gray (Gy) or (rad). 100 rads is the equivalent of 1 Gy (SI unit).
[p009] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 9
[p010] 2.2.2 Dose equivalent
[p010] Linear Energy Transfer (LET) is a term that describes the rate of energy transferred by
[p010] radiation to a medium per unit of length. Radiation with higher LET (alpha or neutron)
[p010] releases a higher rate of energy to tissue than radiation with lower LET (beta, gamma, or x-
[p010] ray). Historically, when calculating the dose equivalent, quality factors termed Q(L) were
[p010] calculated, which corresponded with the differences in radiation LET in tissue. After 1990,
[p010] the International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP) proposed a different
[p010] framework for calculating dose equivalent that incorporated dose over an organ or tissue (T).
[p010] For the radiation quality factor, they developed a radiation weighting factor (w ) that replaced
[p010] Q(L) and that was based on the Biological Effects of Ionizing Radiation (BEIR) VII report.
[p010] Dose equivalent is a measure used to quantify the relative biological effectiveness (RBE) of
[p010] the different types of emitted radiation on human tissue. Radiation with higher LET (i.e.,
[p010] alpha particles and neutrons) causes greater tissue damage than lower LET radiation (i.e.,
[p010] beta particles, x-rays, and gamma rays), and therefore correlates with higher RBE. When
[p010] calculating the dose equivalent, the absorbed dose of an organ/tissue is multiplied by a
[p010] quality factor (w ). The w is a dimensionless factor that compares a radiation's RBE to a
[p010] standard, such as the RBE of gamma rays. Quality factors are listed in Table 1. When
[p010] weighted for the average dose to an organ or tissue, the resultant weighted dose is called the
[p010] organ- or tissue-equivalent dose (H ). Units for dose equivalent are the Sievert (Sv) or
[p010] Roentgen equivalent man (rem); dose equivalent values in diagnostic medicine are commonly
[p010] low and are therefore given in units of millisieverts (mSv) or millirem (mrem).

[p010] Gamma (g) rays 1
[p010] Beta (b) particles 1
[p010] Slow neutrons 5
[p010] Fast neutrons 10
[p010] Alpha (a) particles 20
[p010] 2.2.3 Effective dose
[p010] Effective Dose Equivalent
[p010] The dose equivalent can further be scaled by a tissue weighting factor (w ) that considers the
[p010] relative sensitivity of different organs/tissues to radiation risk of radiocarcinogenesis.
[p010] Effective dose is the multiplication of organ- or tissue-equivalent dose (H ) with w .
[p010] Therefore, the effective dose is the sum of the products of the dose equivalent to the organ or
[p010] tissue (H ) and the weighting factors (w ) applicable to each of the body organs or tissues that
[p010] are irradiated (HE = ΣWTHT). Tissue weighting factors for different tissues are listed in
[p010] Table 2.

[p011] Total Effective Dose Equivalent
[p011] The Total Effective Dose Equivalent (TEDE) is a radiation dosimetry quantity defined by the
[p011] NRC to monitor and control human exposure to ionizing radiation. It is defined differently in
[p011] the NRC regulations and NRC glossary. According to NRC regulations, TEDE is the sum of
[p011] effective dose equivalent from external exposure and from internal exposure (termed
[p011] committed effective dose equivalent), thereby taking into account all known exposures.
[p011] However, the NRC glossary defines it as the sum of the deep-dose equivalent and committed
[p011] effective dose equivalent, which would appear to exclude the effective dose to the skin and
[p011] eyes from nonpenetrating radiation such as beta. These surface doses are included in the
[p011] NRC's shallow dose equivalent, along with contributions from penetrating (gamma)
[p011] radiation.
[p011] • Units for effective dose and TEDE are the Sv or rem (typically mSv or mrem).

[p011] gives a relative scale of risk. Effective dose scales linearly, thus, a PET/CT examination has a
[p011] higher relative risk than a mammogram. (Note, cancer risk is too small to observe below 100
[p011] On average, U.S. inhabitants receive a radiation dose of about 3.1 mSv each year. This dose
[p011] comes from natural background radiation. Most of this background exposure comes from
[p011] radon in the air, with smaller amounts from cosmic rays and the Earth itself. An additional
[p011] 2.2 mSv comes from man-made sources of radiation (mostly exposure from medical
[p011] procedures, and some commercial and industrial sources). In general, a yearly dose of 5.3
[p011] mSv from all radiation sources has not been shown to cause humans any harm. Figure 2

[p013] 2.3 Radiobiology
[p013] There are three general categories of effects resulting from exposure to radiation:
[p013] (1) Genetic or hereditary effects are passed on to the progeny of the exposed individual(s).
[p013] (2) Somatic radiation effects refer to the biological effects of radiation exposure on the cells
[p013] and tissues of the exposed individual. These effects are further classified into two categories:
[p013] tissue (deterministic) and stochastic.
[p013] (3) Teratogenic effects refer to the developmental abnormalities or birth defects that occur in
[p013] a fetus as a result of in utero radiation exposure during pregnancy.
[p013] Radiation destroys cells and tissues through two general pathways, which has implications
[p013] for when and where cells and tissues are most sensitive to radiation:
[p013] (1) Direct pathway - radiation induces single-stranded or double-stranded DNA (ssDNA
[p013] or dsDNA) breaks that lead to cell death.
[p013] a. Higher LET radiation leads to more dsDNA breaks as well as higher
[p013] frequency ssDNA breaks.
[p013] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 13
[p014] b. During the cell cycle, cells are most sensitive to radiation during G2/M when
[p014] the DNA is clumped in tighter bundles and DNA repair mechanisms are not
[p014] active and cannot access the DNA. Contrastingly, cells in late S phase are the
[p014] most radioresistant because of active DNA repair mechanisms.
[p014] c. Cells that are actively dividing are more radiosensitive, while those that are
[p014] not actively dividing are more radioresistant.
[p014] i. Radiosensitive cells include hematopoietic cells (particularly
[p014] lymphocytes), spermatogonia cells, and gastrointestinal stem cells.
[p014] ii. Radioresistant cells include nerve and muscle cells.
[p014] (2) Indirect pathway - radiation induces the development of reactive oxygen species that
[p014] can damage proteins, DNA, and lipids resulting in cell death.
[p014] a. Water is an abundant source of oxygen for radiation injury due to indirect
[p014] effects.
[p014] b. Cells in an oxygen-rich environment are more at risk for the indirect effects of
[p014] radiation.
[p014] 2.3.1 Tissue reactions (deterministic effects)
[p014] Deterministic effects are those for which the severity increases with the dose and for which
[p014] there is a threshold below which no effects are observed. These effects are typically observed
[p014] soon after exposure when the dose exceeds a certain threshold (Table 3).
[p014] Typical tissue effects during medical imaging/radiation therapy include:
[p014] • Skin burns: High doses of radiation can cause erythema (redness), hair loss, blistering,
[p014] and ulceration of the skin.
[p014] • Opacifications and cataracts: Exposure to high doses of radiation can lead to clouding
[p014] of the lens in the eye with delayed onset of years depending on dose and fractionation.

[p014] Stochastic effects are those for which the probability of occurrence increases with the dose,
[p014] but the severity of the effect does not depend on the dose. There is no threshold dose for
[p014] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 14
[p015] stochastic effects; this concept is called the linear no-threshold (LNT) model. The primary
[p015] type of stochastic effect is carcinogenesis and genetic effects.
[p015] 3. Transport and management of radioactive materials
[p015] Materials Transportation
[p015] About 3 million packages of radioactive materials are shipped each year in the U.S., either by
[p015] highway, rail, air, or water. Regulating the safety of these shipments is the joint responsibility
[p015] of the NRC and the Department of Transportation (DOT). The NRC establishes requirements
[p015] for the design and manufacture of packages for radioactive materials. The DOT, which has
[p015] type A and B package requirements, regulates the shipments while they are in transit and sets
[p015] standards for labeling and smaller quantity packages.
[p015] The NRC oversees the safety of the transportation of nuclear materials through a combination
[p015] of regulatory requirements, transportation package certification, inspections, and a system of
[p015] monitoring to ensure that safety requirements are being met.
[p015] Obtaining a Radioactive Material Package Certificate of Compliance
[p015] The NRC must approve type A and B packages used for shipping nuclear material before
[p015] shipment. If the package meets NRC requirements, the NRC issues a Radioactive Material
[p015] Package Certificate of Compliance (CoC) to the organization requesting approval of a
[p015] package. Organizations are authorized to ship radioactive material in a package approved for
[p015] use under the general license provisions.
[p015] For a transportation package to be certified by the NRC, it must be shown by actual test or
[p015] computer analysis to withstand a series of accident conditions. The tests are performed in
[p015] sequence to determine their cumulative effects on the package. The application must address
[p015] the safety and operational characteristics of the package, including design analysis for
[p015] structural and thermal integrity, radiation shielding, nuclear criticality, material content
[p015] confinement, and the conditions listed. In addition, the application must contain operational
[p015] guidance, such as any testing and maintenance requirements, operating procedures, and
[p015] conditions for package use.
[p015] Type A containers are designed to survive normal transportation handling and minor
[p015] accidents. The Type A package is defined as the container and radioactive contents, and most
[p015] radioactive materials in clinical use will be transported in Type A packages. Type B
[p015] packaging is designed to survive severe accidents.
[p015] 3.1. Managing packages
[p015] Colored labels are used to indicate the type and level of hazard contained in a radioactive
[p015] package. Labels rely principally on symbols to indicate the hazard.
[p015] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 15
[p016] Although the package required for transporting radioactive material is based on the
[p016] activity INSIDE the package, the label required on the package is based on the radiation
[p016] hazard OUTSIDE the package. Radioactive material is the only hazardous material that has
[p016] multiple possible labels, depending on the relative radiation levels external to the package,
[p016] with White I, Yellow II, and Yellow III being the most common. Also, labels for radioactive
[p016] material are the only ones that require the shipper to write information on the label. The
[p016] information is a number called the transport index (TI), which is the highest radiation level at
[p016] 1 meter from the surface of the package expressed in mrem/hr. Only the White I label does
[p016] not have a transport index.
[p016] The three labels are commonly called White I, Yellow II, and Yellow III, referring to the
[p016] color of the label and the Roman numeral prominently displayed. A specific label is required
[p016] if the surface radiation limit and the limit at 1 meter satisfy the following requirements (Table

[p017] 3.1.1. Hazard levels and labeling
[p017] The licensee shall ensure that each container of licensed material bears a durable, clearly
[p017] visible label with the radiation symbol and the words "CAUTION, RADIOACTIVE
[p017] MATERIAL" or "DANGER, RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL." The label must also include:
[p017] • Radionuclide(s) present.
[p017] • Quantity of radioactivity.
[p017] • Radioactivity date.
[p017] • Radiation levels.
[p017] • Kinds of materials.
[p017] • Mass enrichment.
[p017] Each licensee shall, prior to removal or disposal of empty uncontaminated containers to
[p017] unrestricted areas, remove or deface the radioactive material label or otherwise clearly
[p017] indicate that the container no longer contains radioactive material.
[p017] 3.1.2. Receipt of radioactive material shipments
[p017] The NRC requires the following procedures when radioactive packages are received and
[p017] opened:
[p017] 1. Each licensee should be able to receive the package or receive notification of the
[p017] arrival of the package at the carrier's terminal and to take possession of the package
[p017] expeditiously.
[p017] 2. Each licensee must monitor the external surfaces of a labeled package for
[p017] radioactive contamination, unless the package contains only radioactive material in
[p017] the form of a gas or is designated as an excepted package.
[p017] 3. Each licensee must monitor all packages known to contain radioactive material for
[p017] radioactive contamination and radiation levels with a Geiger-Mueller detector, and
[p017] for evidence of degradation of package integrity, such as packages that are crushed,
[p017] wet, or damaged. The licensee shall perform the monitoring as soon as practical
[p017] after receipt of the package, but not later than: 3 hours after the package is received
[p017] at the licensee's facility if it is received during the licensee's normal working hours,
[p017] or not later than 3 hours from the beginning of the next working day if it is
[p017] received after working hours.
[p017] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 17
[p018] 4. The licensee shall immediately notify the final delivery carrier and the NRC
[p018] Headquarters Operations Center by telephone if the removable radioactive surface
[p018] contamination exceeds the limits or external radiation levels exceed the limits.
[p018] 6. Each licensee shall establish, maintain, and retain written procedures for safely
[p018] opening packages in which radioactive material is received, and ensure that the
[p018] procedures are followed and that due consideration is given to special instructions
[p018] for the type of package being opened.
[p018] 7. Licensees transferring special form sources in licensee-owned or licensee-operated
[p018] vehicles to and from a work site are exempt from the contamination monitoring
[p018] requirements but are not exempt from the survey requirement for measuring
[p018] radiation levels that is required to ensure that the source is still properly lodged in
[p018] its shield.
[p018] 3.2. Sealed sources
[p018] The NRC and the Agreement States (AS) perform engineering and radiation safety
[p018] evaluations of the ability of sealed sources and devices to safely contain radioactivity under
[p018] the conditions of their possession and use. Sealed sources are radioactive sources that are
[p018] permanently or completely contained either by being bonded to a surface or encased in a
[p018] metal capsule. Sealed sources reduce the risk of dispersion of radioactive material but must
[p018] be monitored in case of damage. Both the NRC and AS issue registration certificates for
[p018] distributors and manufacturers within their jurisdiction. However, only the NRC is
[p018] responsible for devices distributed as exempt products (i.e., smoke detectors, gun sights, etc.)
[p018] and issues those registration certificates.
[p018] Each sealed source must be tested at intervals not to exceed 6 months. In the absence of a
[p018] certificate from a transferor that a test has been made within the 6 months before the transfer,
[p018] the sealed source may not be used until tested. If a sealed source has leaked, a licensee shall
[p018] file a report within 5 days if a required leak test reveals the presence of 185 Bq (0.005 μCi) or
[p018] more of removable contamination. The report must be filed with the appropriate NRC
[p018] Regional Office with a copy to the Director, Office of Nuclear Material Safety and
[p018] Safeguards. The written report must include the model number and serial number, if assigned,
[p018] of the leaking source, the radionuclide and its estimated activity, the results of the test, the
[p018] date of the test, and the corrective action taken.
[p018] Training for use of sealed sources and medical devices for diagnosis requires the authorized
[p018] user of a diagnostic sealed source or a device authorized to be a physician, dentist, or
[p018] podiatrist who is certified by a specialty board whose certification process is recognized by
[p018] the NRC or AS or is an AU. Alternatively, the AU must have completed 8 hours of classroom
[p018] and laboratory training in basic radionuclide handling techniques specifically applicable to
[p018] the use of the device. The training must include radiation physics and instrumentation,
[p018] radiation protection, mathematics pertaining to the use and measurement of radioactivity, and
[p018] radiation biology and has completed training in the use of the device for the uses requested.
[p018] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 18
[p019] 3.3. Exempt quantities
[p019] Examples of exempt quantities include the following:
[p019] • Natural material and ores containing naturally occurring radionuclides in their natural
[p019] state, which are not intended to be processed for the use of these radionuclides.
[p019] • Byproduct material used in products such as electron tubes, self-luminous watches, or
[p019] radiation measuring instruments for standardization or calibration purposes.
[p019] • Small quantities of byproduct material such as in check sources and calibration
[p019] standards for commercial distribution.
[p019] • Use in self-luminous products such as gunsights and exit signs, which contain tiny
[p019] glass vials filled with a radioactive gas such as tritium (hydrogen-3).
[p019] • Use of byproduct material in gas and aerosol detector products such as smoke
[p019] detectors and chemical agent detectors. These products contain tiny foils that provide
[p019] a steady source of ions in analytical chambers. The foils are coated with byproduct
[p019] material such as Am-241 or Ni-63.
[p019] • Capsules containing 1 μCi carbon-14 urea each, for "in vivo" diagnostic use for
[p019] humans.
[p019] 3.4. Use records
[p019] Each licensee shall maintain records showing the results of surveys, calibrations, and sealed
[p019] source leak checks. The licensee shall retain these records for 3 years after the record is
[p019] made.
[p019] The following records are retained for the duration of the license:
[p019] • Results of surveys to determine the dose from external sources and used, in the
[p019] absence of or in combination with individual monitoring data, in the assessment of
[p019] individual dose equivalents.
[p019] • Results of measurements and calculations used to determine individual intakes of
[p019] radioactive material and used in the assessment of internal dose.
[p019] • Results of air sampling, surveys, and required bioassays.
[p019] • Results of measurements and calculations used to evaluate the release of radioactive
[p019] effluents to the environment.
[p019] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 19
[p020] 3.5. Area surveys
[p020] Each licensee shall make or cause to be made surveys of areas, including the subsurface, that
[p020] may be necessary for the licensee to comply with the regulations to evaluate the magnitude
[p020] and extent of radiation levels and the potential radiological hazards of the radiation levels and
[p020] residual radioactivity detected. Records from surveys describing the location and amount of
[p020] subsurface residual radioactivity identified at the site must be kept with records important for
[p020] decommissioning, and such records must be retained. The licensee shall ensure that
[p020] instruments and equipment used for quantitative radiation measurements (e.g., dose rate and
[p020] effluent monitoring) are calibrated periodically for the radiation measured.
[p020] The licensee shall survey with a radiation detection survey instrument (Geiger-Mueller
[p020] meter) at the end of each day of use. A licensee must survey all areas where unsealed
[p020] byproduct material requiring a written directive was prepared for use or administered. A
[p020] licensee must retain a record of each survey required for 3 years. The record must include the
[p020] survey date, the results, the instrument used to make it, and the name of the person who
[p020] performed it.
[p020] 3.6. Waste management/disposal
[p020] A licensee shall dispose of licensed material only by transfer to an authorized recipient, by
[p020] decay in storage, or by release in effluents within the authorized limits. A person must be
[p020] specifically licensed to receive waste containing licensed material from other persons for
[p020] treatment prior to disposal, treatment or disposal by incineration, or decay in storage, or
[p020] disposal at a land disposal facility.
[p020] A licensee may discharge licensed material into sanitary sewerage if each of the following
[p020] conditions is satisfied: The material is readily soluble (or is readily dispersible biological
[p020] material) in water and the quantity of radioactive material released into the sewer in 1 month
[p020] divided by the average monthly volume of water released into the sewer by the licensee does
[p020] not exceed predefined concentration. The total quantity of licensed and other radioactive
[p020] material that the licensee releases into the sanitary sewerage system in a year must not exceed
[p020] 5 curies (185 GBq) of hydrogen-3, 1 curie (37 GBq) of carbon-14, and 1 curie (37 GBq) of
[p020] all other radioactive materials combined.
[p020] Patient excreta from individuals undergoing medical diagnosis or therapy with radioactive
[p020] material are not subject to the limitations.
[p020] Any licensee shipping radioactive waste intended for ultimate disposal at a licensed land
[p020] disposal facility must document the information required on NRC's Uniform Low-Level
[p020] Radioactive Waste Manifest and transfer this recorded manifest information to the intended
[p020] consignee. Each shipment manifest must include a certification by the waste generator.
[p020] A licensee may hold byproduct material with a physical half-life of less than or equal to
[p020] 120 days for decay-in-storage before disposal without regard to its radioactivity if they
[p020] monitor byproduct material with a Geiger-Mueller meter at the surface before disposal and
[p020] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 20
[p021] determine that its radioactivity cannot be distinguished from the background radiation level
[p021] with an appropriate radiation detection survey meter set on its most sensitive scale and with
[p021] no interposed shielding; and if they remove or obliterate all radiation labels, except for
[p021] radiation labels on materials that are within containers and that will be managed as

[p021] 4. Regulatory exposure limits to radioactive materials
[p021] 4.1. Occupational dose limits
[p021] All individuals who, in the course of their employment, are likely to receive a dose of more
[p021] than 100 mrem (1 mSv)/year must receive adequate training to protect themselves against
[p021] radiation. Also, these individuals have the right to know the amount of radiation to which
[p021] they have been exposed. In addition, radiation workers have the right to ask the NRC to
[p021] conduct an inspection if they believe their working environment has safety problems.

[p022] The assigned deep-dose equivalent must be for the part of the body receiving the highest
[p022] exposure. The assigned shallow-dose equivalent must be the dose averaged over the
[p022] contiguous 10 square centimeters of skin receiving the highest exposure. The deep-dose
[p022] equivalent, lens-dose equivalent, and shallow-dose equivalent may be assessed from surveys
[p022] or other radiation measurements for the purpose of demonstrating compliance with the
[p022] occupational dose limits.
[p022] Licensees are required to provide the NRC with an annual report of their workers' individual
[p022] exposures. The NRC, in turn, maintains such radiation exposure data in its Radiation
[p022] Exposure Information and Reporting System (REIRS). Thus, the REIRS database represents
[p022] a resource for use in responding to workers' requests for exposure information and dose
[p022] histories.
[p022] Organizations and individuals may request dose histories from REIRS and may submit
[p022] requests for up to 10 individuals at a time. The requestor is required to enter their name, title,
[p022] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 22
[p023] and contact information, and the identification number, name, and birth date of the monitored
[p023] individual(s).
[p023] 4.1.1. Occupational dose limits for minors
[p023] The annual occupational dose limits for minors are 10 percent of the annual dose limits
[p023] specified for adult workers (see 4.1.).
[p023] 4.2. Pregnant workers
[p023] Other dose limits (additional notes):

[p023] Group Maximum Allowable Dose
[p023] Pregnant workers (embryo/fetus) 0.5 rem (5 mSv) for entire gestation
[p023] Members of the public* 0.1 rem (1 mSv)/year
[p023] Visitor/caregiver/family member 0.5 rem (5 mSv) for duration of contact

[p023] If the dose equivalent to an embryo/fetus is found to have exceeded 0.5 rem (5 mSv) or is
[p023] within 0.05 rem (0.5 mSv) of this dose by the time the worker declares the pregnancy to the

[p023] dose equivalent to the embryo/fetus does not exceed 0.05 rem (0.5 mSv) during the remainder

[p023] A declared pregnant worker is one who has voluntarily informed their employer, in writing,
[p023] of the pregnancy and the estimated date of conception. A separate written declaration should
[p023] be submitted for each pregnancy. A pregnant worker can decide not to declare the

[p023] 4.3. Public (including family and caregivers)
[p023] The total effective dose equivalent to individual members of the public from the licensed
[p023] operation must not exceed 100 mrem (1 mSv) in a year, exclusive of the dose contributions
[p023] from background radiation, any administration the individual has received, exposure to
[p023] individuals administered with radioactive material, and voluntary participation in medical
[p023] research programs.
[p023] If the licensee permits members of the public to have access to controlled areas, the limits for
[p023] members of the public continue to apply to those individuals. A licensee may permit visitors
[p023] to an individual who cannot be released to receive a radiation dose greater than 0.1 rem (1
[p023] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 23
[p024] mSv) if the radiation dose received does not exceed 0.5 rem (5 mSv) and the authorizer user
[p024] has determined before the visit that it is appropriate.
[p024] A licensee or license applicant may apply for prior NRC authorization to operate up to an
[p024] annual dose limit for an individual member of the public of 50 mrem (5 mSv)/year.
[p024] To protect the public, the dose in any unrestricted area from external sources does not exceed
[p024] 2 mrem (0.02 mSv)/hour and 50 mrem (0.5 mSv)/year.
[p024] 4.4. Embryo/fetus (radiation worker)
[p024] 4.5. Individual monitoring
[p024] Each licensee shall monitor exposures to radiation and radioactive material at levels sufficient
[p024] to demonstrate compliance with the occupational dose limits including:
[p024] 1) Adults likely to receive >10% dose limits in 1 year from external sources.
[p024] 2) Minors likely to receive > 100 mrem (1 mSv), a lens dose equivalent in excess of
[p024] 150 mrem (1.5 mSv), or a shallow dose equivalent to the skin or to the extremities in
[p024] excess of 500 mrem (5 mSv) in 1 year from external radiation sources.
[p024] 3) A declared pregnant worker likely to receive > 100 mrem (1 mSv) during the entire
[p024] pregnancy from external radiation sources.
[p024] 4) Individuals entering a high or very high radiation area.
[p024] Each licensee shall maintain records of doses received by all individuals for whom
[p024] monitoring was required. These records must include: the deep-dose equivalent to the whole
[p024] body, lens dose equivalent, shallow-dose equivalent to the skin, shallow-dose equivalent to
[p024] the extremities, the estimated intake of radionuclides, and the effective whole body and organ
[p024] dose.
[p024] The licensee shall make entries of the records at least annually and maintain clear and legible
[p024] records containing all the information. The records required should be protected from public
[p024] disclosure because of their personal privacy nature. The licensee shall maintain the records of
[p024] dose to an embryo/fetus with the records of dose to the declared pregnant worker. The
[p024] declaration of pregnancy shall also be kept on file but may be maintained separately from the
[p024] dose records.
[p024] An RSO monitors readings from radiation badges such that when a radiation worker exceeds
[p024] 10% of the occupational exposure in a quarter, an investigation is conducted to determine the
[p024] cause of the higher exposure. This investigation would include evaluation of adherence to the
[p024] principles of ALARA and proper badge placement.
