[p025] 5. Radiopharmaceutical administration
[p025] Radiopharmaceuticals emit radiation and are used in diagnostic or therapeutic medical
[p025] procedures. Radioisotopes that have short half-lives are generally preferred to minimize the
[p025] radiation dose to the patient and the risk of prolonged exposure. In most cases, these short-
[p025] lived radioisotopes decay to stable elements within minutes, hours, or days, allowing patients
[p025] to be released from the hospital in a relatively short time. The FDA oversees clinical
[p025] administration of radiopharmaceuticals to humans either through an approved New Drug
[p025] Application (NDA) or Abbreviated New Drug Application (ANDA). Investigational drugs
[p025] must also be approved by the FDA for research purposes with an Investigational New Drug
[p025] (IND) application.
[p025] An application for a specific license to manufacture, prepare, or transfer for commercial
[p025] distribution radioactive drugs containing byproduct material for use by persons authorized
[p025] pursuant will be approved if: The manufacturer is registered with the FDA, registered or
[p025] licensed with a state agency as a drug manufacturer, licensed as a pharmacy by a state board
[p025] of pharmacy, or is a PET drug production facility registered with a state agency.
[p025] 5.1. Confirming dosage
[p025] 5.1.1. Labeling
[p025] Each syringe or vial containing unsealed radioactive byproduct material must be labeled to
[p025] identify the radiopharmaceutical. Syringe/vial shields must also be labeled unless the label on
[p025] the syringe/vial is visible when shielded.
[p025] In addition to the above labeling requirements for radioactive materials, radiopharmaceutical
[p025] dosage labels require:
[p025] • The radiopharmaceutical radionuclide.
[p025] • The radiopharmaceutical chemical form.
[p025] • The amount of radiopharmaceutical and the date/time of measurement.
[p025] • The radiopharmaceutical expiration date and time.
[p025] • The quantity of the radiopharmaceutical, including the volume of liquids, the capsule
[p025] number/weight of solids, or the ampules/vials/syringes of gases.
[p025] • The name, address, and telephone number of the dispensing nuclear pharmacy.
[p025] • The prescription or lot number.
[p025] • The name of the radiopharmaceutical.
[p025] When dispensing radiopharmaceuticals, the following must be present:
[p025] • Labels for radiolabeled blood components and radiotherapies must contain the
[p025] patient’s name.
[p025] • A tamper-evident seal must be affixed to the delivery container.
[p025] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 25
[p026] When the patient's name is not available at the time of dispensing for diagnostic dosing, a 72-
[p026] hour exemption is allowed to obtain the name of the patient. No later than 72 hours after
[p026] dispensing the radiopharmaceutical, the patient's name must be associated with the
[p026] prescription in a readily retrievable manner and retained for 3 years.
[p026] The following items must also be included for administered radiopharmaceuticals:
[p026] • Name and address of the nuclear pharmacy.
[p026] • Name of the end authorized user, who is also a prescriber.
[p026] • Lot number of the preparation.
[p026] 5.1.2. Outside of prescribed range for diagnostic and therapeutic dosage
[p026] Before medical use, radiopharmaceutical activities need to be determined and recorded. Unit
[p026] dosages need to be determined by direct measurement of radioactivity or a decay correction
[p026] from a predetermined activity or activity concentration. Other dosages need to be determined
[p026] by direct measurement of radioactivity, measurement of radioactivity and mathematical
[p026] calculations, or a combination of volumetric measurements and mathematical calculations
[p026] from the manufacturers.
[p026] Authorized users can prescribe a diagnostic or therapeutic dosage or dosage range for
[p026] medical use. The administered dosage is permitted to vary from the prescribed dosage by
[p026] ±20%. If the licensee is aware that the administered dosage differs from the prescribed
[p026] dosage by more than 20% and administers it anyway, then the licensee would be cited.
[p026] However, the authorized user can direct the administered dosage to be more than 20% outside
[p026] the prescribed dosage range for medical purposes. In such a situation, the authorized user
[p026] must modify the prescription from one prescribed dosage/dosage range to a revised
[p026] prescribed dosage/dosage range that encompasses the dosage to be administered.
[p026] For a unit dosage, this determination must be made by direct measurement of radioactivity or
[p026] decay correction based on the initial activity determined by a licensed manufacturer. For
[p026] other than unit dosages, this determination must be made by direct measurement of
[p026] radioactivity or a combination of measurement of radioactivity and mathematical calculations
[p026] or volumetric measurements. A licensee shall retain a record of the dosage determination.
[p026] 5.2 Patient identity
[p026] Before any radiopharmaceutical administration, the patient’s identity needs to be confirmed
[p026] with two patient identifiers. Examples of approved patient identifiers include:
[p026] • Individual’s name.
[p026] • Assigned identification number.
[p026] • Telephone number.
[p026] • Date of birth.
[p026] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 26
[p027] • Person-specific identifier.
[p027] • Electronic identification technology coding, such as bar coding or RFID, that includes
[p027] two or more person-specific identifiers.
[p027] In cases where a patient’s identity cannot be verified, a temporary means of identification
[p027] must be used. Formal identification of the patient should occur as soon as possible. When
[p027] obtained, the new identifying information should be used. No standards exist for the use of an
[p027] alias to protect anonymity. Organizations should evaluate risks and utilize two identifiers
[p027] regardless of the alias.
[p027] 5.3. Fetal dose (patient)
[p027] Licensees shall notify the NRC Operations Center by telephone no later than the next
[p027] calendar day after discovery of a dose to an embryo/fetus or nursing child in the following
[p027] situations:
[p027] • Licensees must report any dose to an embryo/fetus if it is greater than 5 rem (50 mSv)
[p027] dose equivalent that results from byproduct administration to a pregnant individual,
[p027] unless it was specifically approved, in advance, by the authorized user.
[p027] • Licensees must report any dose to a nursing child that is a result of administration of
[p027] byproduct material to a breast-feeding individual that is greater than 5 rem (50 mSv)
[p027] total effective dose equivalent or has resulted in unintended permanent functional
[p027] damage to an organ or a physiological system of the child, as determined by a
[p027] physician.
[p027] Licensees shall also submit a written report to the NRC Regional Office within 15 days of
[p027] discovery of dose administration. The written report must include:
[p027] • The licensee’s name.
[p027] • The prescribing physician’s name.
[p027] • A brief description of the event.
[p027] • Why the event occurred.
[p027] • The effect, if any, on the embryo/fetus or the nursing child.
[p027] • What actions, if any, have been taken or are planned to prevent recurrence.
[p027] • Certification that the licensee notified the pregnant individual or mother (or the
[p027] mother’s or child’s responsible relative or guardian) and if not, why not.
[p027] The report must not include the individual’s or child’s name or any other identifying
[p027] information for the individual or child.
[p027] The licensee shall provide notification of the event to the referring provider and also notify
[p027] the pregnant individual or mother no later than 24 hours after discovery of an event that
[p027] would require reporting, unless the referring provider personally informs the licensee either
[p027] that they will inform the mother or that, based on medical judgment, telling the mother would
[p027] be harmful. The licensee is not required to notify the mother without first consulting with the
[p027] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 27
[p028] referring provider. If the referring provider or mother cannot be reached within 24 hours, the
[p028] licensee shall make the appropriate notifications as soon as possible thereafter.
[p028] The licensee may not delay any appropriate medical care for the embryo/fetus or for the
[p028] nursing child, including any necessary remedial care as a result of the event, because of any
[p028] delay in notification. The notification may be made to the mother's or child's responsible
[p028] relative or guardian instead of the mother. If a verbal notification is made, the licensee shall
[p028] inform the mother, or the mother's or child's responsible relative or guardian, that a written
[p028] description of the event can be obtained from the licensee upon request. The licensee shall
[p028] provide such a written description if requested.
[p028] 5.4. Breastfeeding/lactation (patient)
[p028] Breastfeeding mothers may need diagnostic or therapeutic radiopharmaceuticals, leading to
[p028] concerns about radiation exposure to both the mother and child. Radiopharmaceuticals can
[p028] appear in breast milk, causing internal exposure to the child and external exposure from the
[p028] mother.
[p028] Radiation Safety Guidelines
[p028] The ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable) principle is emphasized to minimize radiation
[p028] exposure. Breastfeeding interruption or cessation may be recommended based on the
[p028] radiopharmaceutical and dosage. A nursing mother who has received unsealed byproduct can
[p028] be released if the total effective dose equivalent to any other individual, including the nursing
[p028] child, is projected to not exceed 500 mrem (5 mSv). If the nursing mother continues to
[p028] breastfeed after receiving a radiopharmaceutical and the nursing child’s radiation exposure
[p028] could exceed an effective dose equivalent of 100 mrem (1 mSv), written instructions must be
[p028] given to the mother regarding the potential adverse consequences if breastfeeding is not
[p028] interrupted or ceased as well as guidance on the discontinuation of breastfeeding.
[p028] Before radioiodine therapy, oral and written radiation precaution instructions must be
[p028] provided to the nursing mother and, as needed, to the appropriate family and/or caretakers.
[p028] All concerns and questions should be addressed. The information should be given in a
[p028] sufficient time frame for safety preparation, and at least six weeks prior to the radioiodine
[p028] procedure to allow the necessary time for lactation cessation.
[p028] Recommendations for Specific Radiopharmaceuticals/Radionuclides
[p028] • 18F: 4-hour interruption
[p028] • 67Ga: 28-day interruption
[p028] • 68Ga: No interruption
[p028] • 123I-NaI: 3-day interruption
[p028] • 131I-NaI and 124I-NaI: Permanent cessation for current child. Breastfeeding should be
[p028] stopped six weeks prior to therapy to minimize radiation dose to the breast.
[p028] • 111In-labeled white blood cells: 6-day interruption
[p028] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 28
[p029] • 177Lu-dotatate: Permanent cessation for current child
[p029] • 13N: No interruption
[p029] • 82Rb: No interruption
[p029] • 99mTc-labeled radiopharmaceuticals (all): 24-hour interruption
[p029] • 201Tl-chloride: 4-day interruption
[p029] Brachytherapy and Other Procedures
[p029] Brachytherapy and radioactive seed localization procedures generally require suspension of
[p029] breastfeeding while radioactive seeds are in place, but it can resume after their removal.
[p029] Radioembolic therapy with 90Y-microspheres does not require breastfeeding interruption as
[p029] 90Y does not enter systemic circulation, breast tissue, or breast milk.
[p029] 5.5. Administration of prescribed dosage
[p029] 5.5.1. Safe handling and administration
[p029] Radiopharmaceutical administration for diagnosis or therapy requires a specific area or room
[p029] that is separated from other operations. The area or room should be near the laboratory where
[p029] the radiopharmaceuticals are stored. Transport of radiopharmaceuticals to a patient’s room
[p029] necessitates good radiation safety practice, often necessitating radiation safety staff or nuclear
[p029] technologist staff monitoring and assistance. Physicians, radiation safety, and other allied
[p029] staff should observe principles of ALARA to reduce exposure. These principles include
[p029] holding radioactive sources with tongs or tweezers, informing staff about patient excretion of
[p029] radioactive materials to avoid contamination, avoiding contamination of surfaces, securing
[p029] radioactive materials when staff are not present, not risking oral contamination through any
[p029] worker activities or eating/drinking near radiopharmaceuticals, and conducting training for
[p029] workers. Individuals involved in administering volatile 131I radiopharmaceuticals may be
[p029] sampled for bioassay if > 10 mCi.
[p029] Syringe shields should be used for radiopharmaceutical administration. For positron emitters,
[p029] either lead or tungsten may be employed in a syringe shield. Tungsten is the preferred
[p029] material as it provides better radiation attenuation at a smaller thickness, allowing for a
[p029] lighter and more manageable shield. It is also less toxic than lead. Lead is still used in some
[p029] applications as it can be a more cost-effective option. Disposable gloves, as well as finger and
[p029] body dosimeters should be worn when administering radiopharmaceuticals. Finger (ring)
[p029] dosimeters should be worn under a glove on the hand most likely to be exposed to radiation,
[p029] on a finger exposed to radiation, with the detector (badge) facing the palm.
[p029] Beta emitters can be stopped by lead shielding, but the bremsstrahlung (breaking radiation)
[p029] that will result makes lead an ineffective shielding source as more energy would be created
[p029] from lead interaction. Using lower atomic number material such as acrylic or aluminum
[p029] shielding would be more effective choices for shielding beta emitters.
[p029] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 29
[p030] Criteria for the release of any patient or research subject who received unsealed byproduct
[p030] material or implants with byproduct material is discussed in section 6.5.1.3 of this document.
[p030] After treatment, all articles such as bedding, clothing, towels, food, and food trays should be
[p030] surveyed for contamination before they are removed or held in storage for “Decay-in-
[p030] Storage." The individual should be surveyed before release and instructed on ways to
[p030] minimize contamination and exposure.
[p030] 5.5.2. Shielding for ionizing radiation
[p030] Shielding is an important principle of ALARA. Shielding requires an understanding of
[p030] radionuclides/radiopharmaceuticals and their radiation. Radionuclide generators should be
[p030] placed in a remote, well-shielded enclosure. Dose calibrators should be shielded to protect
[p030] workers. Lead shielding should be used to shield employees and others from procedures with
[p030] 99mTc and other gamma-emitting radiopharmaceuticals. Fume hoods should be used for
[p030] potential airborne radioactive materials.
[p030] For 133Xe studies, additional lead shielding around the charcoal absorber canister, oxygen
[p030] bag, and waste receptacle will prevent exposure. Leakage or contamination from the
[p030] radioactive material should be prevented. Individualized 133Xe room evacuation time
[p030] calculations are required in rooms where ventilation scans are performed.
[p030] Shielded containers or waste cans should be used for syringes and other radioactive waste.
[p030] These containers should be kept at as great a distance as possible from the workers.
[p030] 6. Administrative/practice regulations, responsibilities and training
[p030] 6.1. NRC authority/Agreement States
[p030] Role of NRC and Other Agencies in Regulating the Medical Use of Nuclear Materials
[p030] Radioactive byproduct material is used in calibration sources, radioactive drugs, bone mineral
[p030] analyzers, brachytherapy sources and devices, gamma surgery devices, and teletherapy units
[p030] used in medicine. With limited exceptions, all internal or external administrations of
[p030] byproduct material to human patients or human research subjects must be done in accordance
[p030] with a medical use license. Regulatory authority over the medical use of ionizing radiation is
[p030] shared among several federal, state, and local government agencies.
[p030] The NRC or the responsible Agreement State (AS) has regulatory authority over the
[p030] possession and use of byproduct materials or other nuclear material in medicine. The NRC
[p030] oversees medical uses of nuclear material through licensing, inspection, and enforcement
[p030] programs. The NRC issues medical use licenses to medical facilities and authorized physician
[p030] users, develops guidance and regulations for use by licensees, and maintains a committee of
[p030] medical experts to obtain advice about the use of byproduct materials in medicine.
[p030] The FDA oversees the good practices in the manufacturing of radiopharmaceuticals, medical
[p030] devices, and radiation-producing x-ray machines and accelerators.
[p030] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 30
[p031] Agreement State Program
[p031] The Atomic Energy Act of 1954 allows the NRC to relinquish to an AS portions of its
[p031] regulatory authority to license and regulate byproduct materials. The mechanism for the
[p031] transfer of NRC's authority to a state is an agreement signed by the governor and the chair of
[p031] the commission. The NRC also coordinates with an AS the reporting of event information
[p031] and responses to allegations reported to NRC involving the AS. To date, 39 states have
[p031] entered into agreements with the NRC.
[p031] The Integrated Materials Performance Evaluation Program (IMPEP) toolbox requires the
[p031] NRC to periodically review Agreement States to ensure their radiation control programs are
[p031] adequate to protect public health and safety and compatible with the NRC’s regulatory
[p031] program.
[p031] 6.2. Personnel
[p031] 6.2.1. Radiation safety officer
[p031] A licensee is required to have a radiation safety officer (RSO) with "authority and
[p031] responsibilities for the radiation protection program." The RSO agrees in writing to be
[p031] responsible for implementing the radiation protection program. The licensee, through the
[p031] RSO, verifies that radiation safety activities are being performed in accordance with licensee-
[p031] approved procedures and regulatory requirements. An associate RSO (ARSO) may be
[p031] appointed, in writing, to support the RSO. The RSO, with written agreement of the licensee's
[p031] management, must assign the specific duties and tasks to each ARSO. The RSO shall not
[p031] delegate the authority or responsibilities for implementing the radiation protection program.
[p031] If an RSO or ARSO leaves or ceases to perform their duties, the licensee needs to notify the
[p031] NRC or AS. The RSO or ARSO is certified by a specialty board that is recognized by the
[p031] NRC or AS and each candidate must: hold at least a bachelor's degree from an accredited
[p031] college or university in physical or biological science, have 5 or more years of professional
[p031] experience in health physics, including at least 3 years in applied health physics, and pass an
[p031] exam administered by diplomates of the specialty board.
[p031] The full-time radiation safety experience must involve, among other items: shipping,
[p031] receiving, and performing related radiation surveys; performing checks for proper operation
[p031] of instruments used to determine the activity of dosages, survey meters, and instruments used
[p031] to measure radionuclides; securing and controlling byproduct material; using proper
[p031] decontamination procedures; and disposing of byproduct material.
[p031] A licensee shall provide the RSO sufficient authority, organizational freedom, time,
[p031] resources, and management prerogative, to:
[p031] • Identify radiation safety problems.
[p031] • Initiate, recommend, or provide corrective actions.
[p031] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 31
[p032] • Stop unsafe operations.
[p032] • Verify implementation of corrective actions.
[p032] An authorized user (AU) can be appointed as the RSO for a license if the AU is identified as
[p032] an AU in the license; has experience with the radiation safety aspects of byproduct material
[p032] for which the individual will have RSO responsibilities; has training in the radiation safety,
[p032] regulatory issues, and emergency procedures for which a licensee seeks approval; and has
[p032] obtained written attestation, signed by a preceptor RSO, that the AU has achieved a level of
[p032] radiation safety knowledge sufficient to function independently as an RSO for a medical use
[p032] licensee. Additionally, the AU must agree, in writing, to be responsible for implementing the
[p032] radiation protection program.
[p032] 6.2.2. Authorized user
[p032] An AU is a physician, dentist, or podiatrist who meets criteria and who is specified on the
[p032] license to authorize the medical use of byproduct material. AU applicants must submit
[p032] training and experience information for the purpose(s) for which licensed material will be
[p032] used. The NRC and AS evaluate this information on a case-by-case basis to determine
[p032] whether the training and experience of the identified individuals is appropriate for the
[p032] proposed use(s). A preceptor statement concerning the individuals' completion of appropriate
[p032] training and experience and ability to function independently is a necessary component for
[p032] evaluating the individual's qualifications. Individuals seeking AU recognition for new (not
[p032] previously authorized) medical uses should submit a written attestation from person(s)
[p032] knowledgeable about the radiation safety aspects of the new medical use and the associated
[p032] equipment. A license amendment is not needed to permit the physician to begin work as an
[p032] AU under the NRC license. However, for licensees other than those possessing a Type A
[p032] specific license of broad scope for medical use, a copy of the AS license would have to be
[p032] submitted to the NRC within 30 days.
[p032] Delegation of Supervisory Responsibilities
[p032] Only AUs and authorized nuclear pharmacists (ANPs) are allowed to use or prepare,
[p032] respectively, byproduct material in the practice of medicine. However, an AU or ANP may
[p032] delegate specific tasks associated with the use or preparation of byproduct material in the
[p032] practice of medicine to other individuals who are properly supervised and instructed. The
[p032] AUs and ANPs are best suited for determining tasks that supervised individuals can perform
[p032] and the degree of supervision that each individual needs.
[p032] Approval for Authorized Nuclear Pharmacist, Authorized Medical Physicist, or Authorized
[p032] User
[p032] 6.2.3. Authorized nuclear pharmacist
[p032] An individual can begin duties as an authorized nuclear pharmacist (ANP) as long as one of
[p032] the following three conditions applies: (1) they meet the board certification criteria and
[p032] recentness of training requirements; (2) they are identified as an ANP on an NRC or AS
[p032] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 32
[p033] medical use license or nuclear pharmacy license; or (3) they are identified as an ANP by a
[p033] commercial nuclear pharmacy authorized to identify ANPs.
[p033] The ANP, like the AU, can be selected as an RSO or ARSO if they have experience with the
[p033] radiation safety aspects of similar types of byproduct material for which the individual would
[p033] have RSO responsibilities, similar to the requirements of an AU identified as an RSO. The
[p033] ANP must agree, in writing, to be responsible for implementing the radiation protection
[p033] program. Additionally, the licensee must apply to the NRC for an amendment and submit the
[p033] ANP's training and experience qualifications to serve as RSO.
[p033] 6.2.4. Authorized medical physicist
[p033] An authorized medical physicist (AMP) must be named on licenses authorizing the medical
[p033] uses of 90Sr ophthalmic applicators, teletherapy units, photon-emitting remote after-loader
[p033] units, and gamma stereotactic radiosurgery units. Similar to an AU and ANP, an AMP may
[p033] be identified as an RSO following a similar pattern of requirements as an ANP.
[p033] 6.2.5. Radiation safety committee
[p033] Licensees who are authorized for two or more different types of uses of byproduct material
[p033] must have a radiation safety committee (RSC) to oversee all uses of byproduct material
[p033] permitted by the license. The RSC must include an AU of each type of use permitted by the
[p033] license, the RSO, a representative of the nursing service, and a representative of management
[p033] who is neither an AU nor an RSO. The committee may include other members the licensee
[p033] considers appropriate.
[p033] The RSC has the authority and responsibilities for the radiation protection program including
[p033] but not limited to evaluating requests for a license application, renewal, or amendment before
[p033] submission to the NRC and allowing an individual to work as an AU, ANP, or AMP.
[p033] 6.3 Radioactive Materials (RAM) License
[p033] The NRC issues specific licenses for possession and use of byproduct, source, and special
[p033] nuclear material. After the initial license is granted, it may be modified during an amendment
[p033] or renewal to reflect changes in circumstances affecting the operations after the license was
[p033] issued. A company/facility that wishes to obtain a license to use nuclear materials must
[p033] submit an application to the NRC. This application must demonstrate how the use of these
[p033] materials will meet NRC safety requirements. Applicants must provide information on the
[p033] type, form, and intended quantity of available facilities, qualifications of users, and radiation
[p033] protection programs. The license may contain certain NRC conditions agreed to by the
[p033] licensee.
[p033] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 33
[p034] 6.3.1. Broad scope license
[p034] Type A: Type A licenses of broad scope are typically the largest licensed programs and
[p034] encompass a broad range of uses. Type A broad scope licensees use an RSC, RSO, and
[p034] criteria developed and submitted by the licensee. These licenses typically exist at a large
[p034] academic center.
[p034] Type B: Type B broad scope licensed programs are normally smaller and less diverse than
[p034] Type A broad scope programs. Type B broad scope licensees use an RSO and criteria
[p034] developed and submitted by the licensee
[p034] Type C: Type C broad scope licensed programs are typically issued to institutions that do not
[p034] require significant quantities of radioactive material but need the flexibility to possess a
[p034] variety of different radioactive materials. Users of licensed material under these programs are
[p034] approved by the licensee based on training and experience criteria. These licenses usually
[p034] exist for nuclear cardiology clinics or small research labs requiring small quantities of
[p034] radioactive material.
[p034] 6.3.2. NRC Master Materials License
[p034] A Master Materials License (MML) is a license issued by the NRC to federal organizations
[p034] authorizing use of byproduct material at multiple sites that fall under the jurisdiction of the
[p034] federal agency for industrial and medical purposes. The MML allows the federal agency to
[p034] conduct some activities as a regulator, such as issuing permits for radioactive materials use at
[p034] the sites that use byproduct materials, conducting inspections, handling allegations, following
[p034] up on incidents and events, and taking enforcement actions. The NRC, in turn, provides
[p034] oversight of MML licensees and inspects MMLs at least every two years to evaluate how
[p034] well the licensee is implementing their program.
[p034] 6.4. Written directive
[p034] “Written directive” refers to an AU's written order for the administration of byproduct
[p034] material or radiation from byproduct material to a specific patient or human research subject.
[p034] 6.4.1. Uptake, dilution, and excretion studies
[p034] Use of unsealed byproduct material for uptake, dilution, and excretion studies do not require
[p034] a written directive. A licensee may use any unsealed byproduct material prepared for medical
[p034] use for uptake, dilution, or excretion studies that is obtained from a licensed manufacturer, a
[p034] PET radioactive drug producer, or prepared by an ANP or AU with specific training
[p034] approved by the NRC. Approved Radioactive Drug Research Committee (RDRC) or
[p034] Investigational New Drug (IND) byproduct material also does not require a written directive
[p034] when used for uptake, dilution, and excretion studies.
[p034] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 34
[p035] The licensee shall require an AU of unsealed byproduct material for uptake, dilution, and
[p035] excretion studies to be a physician and have the following training: 60 hours of training and
[p035] experience in basic radionuclide handling techniques and radiation safety applicable to the
[p035] medical use of unsealed byproduct material for uptake, dilution, and excretion studies
[p035] (including a minimum of 8 hours of classroom and laboratory training). Candidates must pass
[p035] an exam, administered by diplomates of the specialty board, that assesses knowledge and
[p035] competence in radiation safety, radionuclide handling, and quality control.
[p035] Classroom and laboratory training must be inclusive of the following areas: radiation physics
[p035] and instrumentation, radiation protection, mathematics pertaining to the use and measurement
[p035] of radioactivity, chemistry of byproduct material for medical use, and radiation biology. AU
[p035] candidates must also demonstrate work experience, under the supervision of an AU who
[p035] meets the NRC or AS requirements, including ordering, receiving, and unpacking radioactive
[p035] materials safely and performing the related radiation surveys; performing quality control
[p035] procedures on instruments used to determine the activity of dosages and performing checks
[p035] for proper operation of survey meters; calculating, measuring, and safely preparing patient or
[p035] human research subject dosages; using administrative controls to prevent a medical event
[p035] involving the use of unsealed byproduct material; using procedures to contain spilled
[p035] byproduct material safely and using proper decontamination procedures; and administering
[p035] dosages of radioactive drugs to patients or human research subjects. AU candidates must also
[p035] have a written attestation that they have satisfactorily completed the training and work
[p035] requirements from either a preceptor AU or a residency program director who affirms in
[p035] writing that the attestation represents the consensus of the residency program faculty where at
[p035] least one faculty member is an AU.
[p035] 6.4.2. Imaging and localization studies
[p035] Use of unsealed byproduct material for imaging and localization studies for which a written
[p035] directive is not required
[p035] Except for quantities that require a written directive, a licensee may use any unsealed
[p035] byproduct material prepared for medical use for imaging and localization studies that is
[p035] obtained from a licensed manufacturer, a PET radioactive drug producer, or prepared by an
[p035] ANP or AU with specific training approved by the NRC. Alternatively, medical use for
[p035] imaging and localization studies for use in research in accordance with a Radioactive Drug
[p035] Research Committee (RDRC) approved protocol or an Investigational New Drug (IND)
[p035] protocol accepted by the FDA do not require a written directive.
[p035] Training for imaging and localization studies
[p035] The licensee shall require an AU of unsealed byproduct material for imaging and localization
[p035] studies to be a physician who (1) is certified by a medical specialty board whose certification
[p035] process is recognized by the NRC or AS; (2) has completed 700 hours of training and
[p035] experience in basic radionuclide handling techniques (including a minimum of 80 hours of
[p035] classroom and laboratory training) and radiation safety applicable to the medical use of
[p035] unsealed byproduct material for imaging and localization studies; and (3) passes an exam,
[p035] administered by diplomates of the specialty board, that assesses knowledge and competence
[p035] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 35
[p036] in radiation safety, radionuclide handling, and quality control similar to the listed
[p036] requirements for uptake, dilution, and excretion studies. Classroom and laboratory training is
[p036] inclusive of the same areas for uptake, dilution, and excretion studies.
[p036] 6.4.3. Unsealed byproduct material requiring written directive
[p036] Use of unsealed byproduct material for which a written directive is required
[p036] A licensee may use any unsealed byproduct material prepared for medical use and for which
[p036] a written directive is required that is obtained from a licensed manufacturer following NRC
[p036] or equivalent AS requirements, excluding production of PET radionuclides, prepared by an
[p036] ANP or AU or an individual under supervision of an ANP or AU.
[p036] Required training for use of unsealed byproduct material for which a written directive is
[p036] required includes certification by a medical specialty board whose certification process has
[p036] been recognized by the NRC or AS, and that person is a physician who successfully
[p036] completed residency training in a radiation therapy or nuclear medicine training program or a
[p036] program in a related medical specialty. These residency training programs must include 700
[p036] hours (including 200 classroom hours) of training and experience, and graduates must pass an
[p036] exam, administered by diplomates of the specialty board, that tests knowledge and
[p036] competence in radiation safety, radionuclide handling, quality assurance, and clinical use of
[p036] unsealed byproduct material for which a written directive is required.
[p036] In addition to the areas discussed under imaging and localization studies, training for use of
[p036] unsealed byproduct material for which a written directive is required includes safely
[p036] preparing patient or human research subject dosages; administrative controls to prevent a
[p036] medical event involving the use of unsealed byproduct material; procedures to contain spilled
[p036] byproduct material safely and using proper decontamination procedures; and administering
[p036] dosages of radioactive drugs to patients or human research subjects.
[p036] Work experience must involve a minimum of three cases in each of the following categories
[p036] for which the individual is requesting AU status:
[p036] (1) Oral administration of less than or equal to 1.22 GBq (33 mCi) of sodium iodide 131I, for
[p036] which a written directive is required;
[p036] (2) Oral administration of greater than 1.22 GBq (33 mCi) of sodium iodide 131I;
[p036] (3) Parenteral administration of any radioactive drug that contains a radionuclide that is
[p036] primarily used for its electron emission, beta radiation characteristics, alpha radiation
[p036] characteristics, or photon energy of less than 150 keV, for which a written directive is
[p036] required.
[p036] The AU candidate must have written attestation that the individual has satisfactorily
[p036] completed the training and is able to independently fulfill the radiation safety-related duties
[p036] as an AU for the medical uses for which the individual is requesting AU status from either a
[p036] preceptor AU who meets the requirements from the NRC or equivalent AS requirements, or a
[p036] residency program director who affirms in writing that the attestation represents the
[p036] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 36
[p037] consensus of the residency program faculty where at least one faculty member is an AU who
[p037] meets the requirements.
[p037] 6.5. Radiopharmaceutical therapy
[p037] A negative serum beta-hCG pregnancy test must be documented in the patient's chart for all
[p037] potentially childbearing individuals who are under 50 years old when receiving therapeutic
[p037] radiopharmaceuticals. The pregnancy test must be within one week of the sodium iodide 131I
[p037] therapy. A pregnancy test is not necessary if the patient has had a remote bilateral tubal
[p037] ligation or hysterectomy.
[p037] Training for the oral administration of sodium iodide 131I requiring a written directive in
[p037] quantities less than or equal to 1.22 GBq (33 mCi)
[p037] The licensee shall require a written directive dated and signed by an AU for the oral
[p037] administration of all 131I quantities greater than 1.11 MBq (30 μC). Additionally, 131I
[p037] quantities less than or equal to 1.22 GBq (33 mCi) must have a written directive by a
[p037] physician who is certified by a medical specialty board whose certification process has been
[p037] recognized by the NRC or an AS, or has successfully completed 80 hours of classroom and
[p037] laboratory training, applicable to the medical use of sodium iodide 131I for procedures
[p037] requiring a written directive.
[p037] Training for the oral administration of sodium iodide 131I requiring a written directive in
[p037] quantities greater than 1.22 GBq (33 mCi)
[p037] The licensee shall require an authorized user for the oral administration of sodium iodide 131I
[p037] requiring a written directive in quantities greater than 1.22 GBq (33 mCi) to be a physician
[p037] who fulfils the same requirements as that for less than 1.22 GBq (33 mCi).
[p037] 6.5.1.1 Inpatient
[p037] A patient treated with therapeutic ionizing radiation as an inpatient should be treated in a
[p037] room with appropriate shielding, such as lead or concrete. Where shielding is not available, a
[p037] corner, private hospital room not next to a stairwell or open area is optimal. The head of the
[p037] bed should be positioned against the outside wall. Additional protection can be achieved by
[p037] placing a shield between the door and the patient.
[p037] Before a patient is dosed, the room should be prepared. Signage indicating a radiation hazard
[p037] and “no housekeeping services” should be placed on the door. Linen and trash hampers are
[p037] placed inside the room. All surfaces the patient may frequently touch, such as the toilet,
[p037] faucet handles, phone, and bedrails, should be wrapped in plastic. Materials used in the care
[p037] of the patient are left in the room for the entire hospital stay.
[p037] As much as possible, healthcare workers and visitors should minimize their contact with the
[p037] patient’s body fluids. To minimize fluid exposure, managing nausea and preventing emesis,
[p037] which are common side effects of sodium iodide 131I therapy, are important. Hospitals may
[p037] ask patients receiving sodium iodide 131I to handle their own urine collection and
[p037] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 37
[p038] measurement, so that staff members need not have close contact with the fluid. Male patients
[p038] should be instructed to sit while voiding, and all patients should be instructed to flush the
[p038] toilet twice, with the lid closed, to reduce the risk of aerosolization. Vigorous hydration is
[p038] highly encouraged because it hastens biologic elimination. Because sweat is a source of
[p038] contamination, patients can be encouraged to take frequent showers as tolerated for the first
[p038] two weeks after treatment.
[p038] In the event of cardiac arrest or other medical emergencies, staff should follow the facility’s
[p038] specific protocols. When a patient receiving sodium iodide 131I is at immediate risk of death
[p038] or severe harm, many facilities relax their radiation restrictions and allow the patient to be
[p038] moved away from the shielded room. When administering cardiopulmonary resuscitation to a
[p038] radioactive patient, staff members should be cautious and use appropriate barriers.
[p038] Visitor safety is promoted by prohibiting pregnant visitors and visitors younger than 18 years.
[p038] State regulations and hospital policies vary, but visits typically are limited to 30 minutes and
[p038] require that visitors maintain a distance of at least 6 feet from the patient.
[p038] The patient and room should be surveyed with dosimeters at prescribed points at least daily.
[p038] Surveys include points where care is provided, such as 1 meter from the patient’s chest, each
[p038] side of the bed, the foot of the bed, and the doorway. In addition, the visitor chair, the inside
[p038] and outside of the door, and the adjacent patient wall should be surveyed. When survey
[p038] readings at 1 meter from the patient’s chest are 7 mrem per hour or less, the patient is
[p038] considered generally safe for discharge. However, the discharge criteria may vary from state
[p038] to state and from facility to facility.
[p038] 6.5.1.2. Outpatient
[p038] NRC regulations allow sodium iodide 131I patients to be released following treatment when
[p038] the radiation dose to third parties is not likely to exceed 500 mrem (5 mSv). (An average
[p038] person receives about 300 mrem [3 mSv] each year from natural and background radiation.)
[p038] The regulations assume the dose would apply principally to the patient’s family or other
[p038] caregivers during the first few days the patient spends at home following treatment.
[p038] Accordingly, treating physicians are required to consider the patient’s living conditions and
[p038] provide instructions for avoiding unnecessary exposure to others.
[p038] The NRC strongly discourages medical licensees who conduct outpatient treatment of thyroid
[p038] patients with sodium iodide 131I from recommending patients stay at hotels immediately after
[p038] treatment.
[p038] The NRC also reminds treating physicians to inquire about the patient’s intended destination
[p038] following treatment so that appropriate instructions may be given on how to manage
[p038] exposure to others. If a patient is adamant about not being hospitalized or not going to a
[p038] private residence and plans to go to an alternative location such as a hotel, treating physicians
[p038] must still provide adequate instructions on how the patient can keep radiation doses to others
[p038] as low as possible.
[p038] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 38

[p040] 6.5.2. Parenteral therapy (alpha, beta)
[p040] Training for the parenteral administration of unsealed byproduct material requiring a written
[p040] directive
[p040] The licensee shall require an AU for the parenteral administration requiring a written
[p040] directive to be a physician who is an AU or maintains equivalent AS requirements, or,
[p040] alternatively, a physician certified by a medical specialty board whose certification process
[p040] has been recognized by the NRC or AS and who has successfully completed training
[p040] applicable to parenteral and work experience under the supervision of an AU who meets the
[p040] requirements for parenteral administrations.
[p040] The work experience must involve ordering, receiving, and unpacking radioactive materials
[p040] safely and performing the related radiation surveys; performing quality control procedures on
[p040] instruments used to determine the activity of dosages and performing checks for proper
[p040] operation of survey meters; calculating, measuring, and safely preparing patient or human
[p040] research subject dosages; using administrative controls to prevent a medical event involving
[p040] the use of unsealed byproduct material; using procedures to contain spilled byproduct
[p040] material safely and using proper decontamination procedures; and administering dosages to
[p040] patients or human research subjects that include at least three cases of the parenteral
[p040] administrations.
[p040] Also required is written attestation that the individual is able to independently fulfill the
[p040] radiation safety-related duties as an AU for the parenteral administration of unsealed
[p040] byproduct material requiring a written directive. The attestation must be obtained from either
[p040] a preceptor AU or a residency program director who affirms in writing that the attestation
[p040] represents the consensus of the residency program faculty where at least one faculty member
[p040] is an AU.
[p040] 6.6. Radiopharmacy ("hot lab")
[p040] Licensed materials must be secured, either by storage in a locked room (such as the nuclear
[p040] pharmacy or “hot lab”) or by constant surveillance (such as in imaging rooms in which
[p040] patient dosages may be located). The hot lab is used to receive, store, and/or prepare
[p040] radiopharmaceuticals that are administered to patients for nuclear radiology studies.
[p040] An important component of the hot lab is the dose calibrator, which measures radioactivity in
[p040] vials, syringes, capsules, etc. The calibrator is often placed behind a lead or leaded-glass
[p040] shield to minimize radiation exposure to the individual while they are working with
[p040] radioactivity. Patient dosages and shielded vials are also generally placed and/or stored
[p040] behind this shield.
[p040] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 40
[p041] 6.6.1. Safe procedures
[p041] Safety instruction
[p041] A licensee shall provide radiation safety instruction, initially and at least annually, to
[p041] personnel caring for patients or human research subjects who cannot be released. To satisfy
[p041] this requirement, the instruction must include patient or human research subject control;
[p041] visitor control, including routine visitation to hospitalized individuals; contamination and
[p041] waste control; notification of the RSO or their designee; and an AU if the patient or the
[p041] human research subject has a medical emergency or dies. A licensee shall retain a record of
[p041] individuals receiving instruction.
[p041] Safety precautions
[p041] For each patient or human research subject who cannot be released, a licensee shall quarter
[p041] the patient or the human research subject either in a private room with a private sanitary
[p041] facility or with another individual who also has received therapy with unsealed byproduct
[p041] material and who also cannot be released. Also, the licensee must visibly post the patient's or
[p041] the human research subject's room with a “Radioactive Materials” sign, note on the door or in
[p041] the patient's or human research subject's chart specifying where and how long visitors may
[p041] stay in the patient's or the human research subject's room. The licensee must either monitor
[p041] material and items removed from the patient's or the human research subject's room to
[p041] determine that their radioactivity cannot be distinguished from the natural background
[p041] radiation level or handle the material and items as radioactive waste. The licensee shall notify
[p041] the RSO, or their designee, and the AU as soon as possible if the patient or human research
[p041] subject has a medical emergency or dies.
[p041] 6.6.2. Generator systems
[p041] Eluting the generator
[p041] The procedures for elution are designed to provide a sterile, pyrogen-free product suitable for
[p041] human injection or for compounding into other radiopharmaceutical preparations. Because
[p041] the design of individual commercial generators varies, the precise methods used for their
[p041] elution will also vary.
[p041] 6.6.2.2. Quality control
[p041] Permissible molybdenum-99, strontium-82, and strontium-85 concentrations
[p041] A licensee may not administer to humans a radiopharmaceutical that contains:
[p041] • More than 0.15 kBq of 99Mo per MBq of 99mTc (0.15 uCi of 99Mo per mCi of 99mTc);
[p041] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 41
[p042] • More than 0.02 kBq of 82Sr per MBq of 82Rb chloride injection (0.02 uCi of 82Sr per
[p042] mCi of 82Rb chloride); or
[p042] • More than 0.2 kBq of 85Sr per MBq of 82Rb chloride injection (0.2 uCi of 85Sr per
[p042] mCi of 82Rb).
[p042] The eluates from each generator should be measured to demonstrate compliance. For
[p042] 82Sr/82Rb generators, the concentration of radionuclides 82Sr and 85Sr should be measured
[p042] before the first patient of the day. The licensee shall report by telephone any measurement
[p042] within 7 calendar days after discovery that an eluate exceeded the permissible concentration
[p042] listed.
[p042] The licensee shall submit a written report to the NRC within 30 calendar days after discovery
[p042] of an eluate exceeding the permissible concentration at the time of generator elution. The
[p042] written report must include the action taken by the licensee, the patient dose assessment, the
[p042] methodology used to make this dose assessment if the eluate was administered to a person,
[p042] and the probable cause and an assessment of failure in the licensee's equipment, procedures
[p042] or training that contributed to the excessive readings if an error occurred in the licensee's
[p042] breakthrough determination.
[p042] 68Ga can be produced in a cyclotron or by the elution of a 68Ge/68Ga generator. 68Ge/68Ga
[p042] generators are similar to conventional 99Mo/99mTc and 82Sr/82Rb generators. Like 99Mo/99mTc
[p042] and 82Sr/82Rb generators, breakthrough of the parent radionuclide is possible when eluting the
[p042] generator. This could lead to 68Ge contaminating the 68Ga radiopharmaceutical and
[p042] potentially causing an unnecessarily high radiation exposure to patients. However, no similar
[p042] limit of breakthrough is specified for 68Ge/68Ga generators and applicants must commit
[p042] individually to safety protocols to the NRC.
[p042] 6.6.3. Recordkeeping
[p042] Each record required must be legible throughout the specified retention period. The record
[p042] may be the original or a reproduced copy. The record may be stored in electronic media with
[p042] the capability for producing legible, accurate, and complete records during the required
[p042] retention period. Records, such as letters, drawings, and specifications, must include all
[p042] pertinent information, such as stamps, initials, and signatures. The licensee shall maintain
[p042] adequate safeguards against tampering with and loss of records.
[p042] Each licensee shall report by telephone to the NRC any lost, stolen, or missing licensed
[p042] material that becomes known to the licensee if it appears to the licensee that an exposure
[p042] could result to persons in unrestricted areas. Each licensee is required to make a written
[p042] report within 30 days after making the telephone report setting forth the following
[p042] information:
[p042] • A description of the licensed material involved, including kind, quantity, and
[p042] chemical and physical form.
[p042] • A description of the circumstances under which the loss or theft occurred.
[p042] • A statement of disposition, or probable disposition, of the licensed material involved.
[p042] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 42
[p043] • Exposures of individuals to radiation, circumstances under which the exposures
[p043] occurred, and the possible total effective dose equivalent to persons in unrestricted
[p043] areas.
[p043] • Actions that have been taken, or will be taken, to recover the material.
[p043] • Procedures or measures that have been, or will be, adopted to ensure against a
[p043] recurrence of the loss or theft of licensed material.
[p043] Each licensee shall submit a written report within 30 days after learning of any of the
[p043] following occurrences: doses in excess of any of the occupational dose limits for adults or
[p043] minors, the limits for an embryo/fetus of a declared pregnant individual, or the limits for an
[p043] individual member of the public.
[p043] Each licensee shall submit a written report within 30 days after learning of excesses of
[p043] radiation air emissions, or excessive radiation in a restricted space, or 10 times the limits of
[p043] an unrestricted area.
[p043] Reports should include individual dose estimates; levels of radiation involved; the cause of
[p043] the elevated exposures, dose rates, or concentrations; and corrective steps taken or planned to
[p043] ensure against a recurrence. For occupationally overexposed individuals, their name, Social
[p043] Security account number, and date of birth should be included and clearly labeled with
[p043] "Privacy Act Information: Not for Public Disclosure."
[p043] Records of authority and responsibilities for radiation protection programs
[p043] A licensee shall retain a record of actions for 5 years for the RSO and radiation program
[p043] changes. The record must include a summary of the actions taken and a signature of licensee
[p043] management. The licensee shall retain a copy of the duties and responsibilities of the RSO
[p043] and must include a copy of the previous and current radiation safety procedures.
[p043] Records of written directives
[p043] A licensee shall retain a copy of each written directive for 3 years.
[p043] Records of calibrations of instruments used to measure the activity of unsealed byproduct
[p043] material
[p043] A licensee shall maintain a record of instrument and radiation survey calibrations for 3 years.
[p043] The records must include the model and serial number of the instrument, the date of the
[p043] calibration, the results of the calibration, and the name of the person who performed the
[p043] calibration.
[p043] Records of dosages of unsealed byproduct material for medical use
[p043] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 43
[p044] A licensee shall maintain a record of dosage determinations for 3 years. The record must
[p044] contain the radiopharmaceutical; the patient's name or identification number; the prescribed
[p044] dosage; the determined dosage including date and time or a notation that the total activity is
[p044] less than 1.1 MBq (30 μCi); and the name of the person who determined the dosage.
[p044] Records of leaks tests and inventory of sealed sources and brachytherapy sources
[p044] A licensee shall retain records of leak tests for 3 years. The records must include the model
[p044] number and serial number if one has been assigned; the identity of each source by
[p044] radionuclide and its estimated activity; the results of the test; and the name of the person who
[p044] performed the test. A licensee shall retain records of the semi-annual physical inventory of
[p044] sealed sources and brachytherapy sources for 3 years.
[p044] Records of surveys for ambient radiation exposure rate
[p044] A licensee shall retain a record of each survey required for 3 years. The record must include
[p044] the date of the survey; the results of the survey; the instrument used to make the survey; and
[p044] the name of the person who performed the survey.
[p044] Records of the release of individuals containing unsealed byproduct material or implants
[p044] containing byproduct material
[p044] A licensee shall retain a record for 3 years of the basis for authorizing the release of an
[p044] individual if the total effective dose equivalent is calculated by using the retained activity,
[p044] rather than the activity administered, using an occupancy factor less than 0.25 at 1 meter, or
[p044] using the biological or effective half-life.
[p044] A licensee shall retain a record for 3 years that the instructions were provided to a
[p044] breastfeeding individual if the radiation dose to the infant or child from continued
[p044] breastfeeding could result in a total effective dose equivalent exceeding 5 mSv (0.5 rem).
[p044] 7. Emergency procedures, accidents/incidents, special circumstances
[p044] This section covers medical/reportable events as defined by the NRC. It also addresses spills
[p044] of radioactive materials and radiation accidents/incidents/disasters including “dirty bombs.”
[p044] 7.1 Medical events/reportable events
[p044] The term “medical event” may sound alarming when used in nuclear radiology and radiation
[p044] therapy. But it may not mean a patient has been harmed. It means there may have been a
[p044] problem in a medical facility’s use of radioactive materials. These materials can help to
[p044] diagnose and treat illnesses or be used in medical research. Small amounts can allow
[p044] physicians to see certain organs. Images created using radioactive materials can help to find,
[p044] identify, and measure tumors, or view problems in an organ’s structure or function.
[p044] Radioactive materials can also kill cancerous tissue, shrink a tumor, or reduce pain.
[p044] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 44
[p045] NRC regulations aim to ensure that radioactive materials are used properly. This is also true
[p045] when they are used in medical diagnosis, treatment, or research. The rules protect the safety
[p045] of patients, medical workers, the public, and the environment. They define “medical event” in
[p045] very specific terms.
[p045] A “medical event” involving radioactive materials occurs if BOTH:
[p045] 1. The dosage given differs from the prescribed dose or dose that would have resulted
[p045] from the prescribed dosage by more than 5 rem (0.05 Sv) effective dose equivalent, 50
[p045] rem (0.5 Sv) to an organ or tissue, or 50 rem (0.5 Sv) shallow dose equivalent to the
[p045] skin. AND
[p045] 2. One or more of the following incidents occur:
[p045] • The dosage given is off by at least 20% from the prescribed dosage, either too
[p045] high or too low.
[p045] • The wrong drug is used.
[p045] • The drug is given by the wrong route.
[p045] • The wrong individual receives the dosage.
[p045] • A dosage is administered to the wrong part of the body and exceeds by 50% or
[p045] more the dosage that area should have received.
[p045] • A sealed source used in the treatment leaks.
[p045] The NRC requires reporting because a medical event shows the licensee had a problem in
[p045] administering the physician’s (AU’s) prescription. The problem may have been technical or a
[p045] quality assurance issue. Dosage errors may be a sign of problems in the medical facility’s
[p045] operations. But there is no scientific basis to conclude that such an error results in harm to a
[p045] patient.
[p045] To know if a patient has been harmed, the physician (AU) must do more analysis. Harm may
[p045] come from too high a dosage. But it could also result from inadequate treatment when a
[p045] dosage is too low.
[p045] The NRC also analyzes each event to see if further action is needed. If there is a violation of
[p045] the regulations, the NRC might take enforcement action. The agency will also look for trends
[p045] to see if something in NRC regulations or guidance may need to be clarified.
[p045] Severe events are rare. An example would be a dosage error that is well over 20% too high or
[p045] too low. In severe events, an independent medical consultant will assess the patient’s risk of
[p045] harm.
[p045] In all cases, a “medical event” indicates potential problems in a facility’s use of radioactive
[p045] materials. It does not necessarily result in harm to a patient. But the NRC’s rules are designed
[p045] so events are reported to the NRC and the public. Even when there is no harm to a patient, the
[p045] NRC protects the public health and safety.
[p045] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 45
[p046] 7.2 Radiation spills
[p046] The decision to implement a major spill procedure instead of a minor spill procedure
[p046] depends on many incident-specific variables, such as the number of individuals affected,
[p046] other hazards present, likelihood of contamination spread, and types of surfaces contaminated
[p046] as well as the radiotoxicity of the spilled material. For some spills of short-lived
[p046] radionuclides, the best spill procedure may be restricted access pending complete decay.
[p046] To determine whether a major or minor spill procedure should be implemented, take the
[p046] following steps, using Table 8 for guidance:
[p046] • Estimate the amount of radioactivity spilled.
[p046] • Initiate a major or minor spill procedure based on the listed dividing line.
[p046] • Spills above these millicurie amounts are considered major, below are considered
[p046] minor.
[p046] • Note that some spills require evacuation of the area.

[p046] 7.2.1 Major spill – response and handling
[p046] A. CLEAR THE AREA: Notify all persons not involved in the spill to vacate the room.
[p046] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 46
[p047] B. PREVENT THE SPREAD: Cover the spill with absorbent paper, but do not attempt to
[p047] clean it up. To prevent the spread of contamination, limit the movement of all personnel who
[p047] may be contaminated.
[p047] C. SHIELD THE SOURCE: If possible, shield the spill. This should be done only if it can be
[p047] done without further contamination or a significant increase in radiation exposure.
[p047] D. CLOSE THE ROOM: Lock or otherwise secure the area to prevent entry.
[p047] E. CALL FOR HELP: Notify the RSO immediately.
[p047] F. PERSONNEL DECONTAMINATION: Remove contaminated clothing and flush
[p047] contaminated skin with lukewarm water and then wash with mild soap. If contamination
[p047] remains, induce perspiration by covering the area with plastic. Then wash the affected area
[p047] again to remove any contamination that was released by the perspiration.
[p047] G. CLEANUP: The RSO will supervise the cleanup of the spill and will complete the
[p047] Radioactive Spill Report and the Radioactive Spill Contamination Survey report forms.
[p047] 7.2.2 Minor spill – response and handling
[p047] A. NOTIFY: Notify persons in the area that a spill has occurred.
[p047] B. PREVENT THE SPREAD: Cover the spill with absorbent paper.
[p047] C. CLEAN UP: Use disposable gloves and absorbent paper. Carefully fold the absorbent
[p047] paper with the clean side out and place in a labeled plastic bag for transfer to a radioactive
[p047] waste container. Also put contaminated gloves and any other contaminated disposable
[p047] material in the bag.
[p047] D. SURVEY: Check the area around the spill with a low-range radiation detection survey
[p047] meter. Also check your hands, clothing, and shoes for contamination.
[p047] E. REPORT: Report the incident to the RSO.
[p047] F. DOCUMENT: Complete the Radioactive Spill Report and the Radioactive Contamination
[p047] Survey forms.
[p047] 7.2.3 Surface contamination limits
[p047] The amount of removable radioactive material per 100 cm2 of surface area should be
[p047] determined by wiping that area with a filter or soft absorbent paper, applying moderate
[p047] pressure, and assessing the amount of radioactive material on the wipe with an appropriate
[p047] instrument of known efficiency. When removable contamination on objects of less surface
[p047] area is determined, the pertinent levels should be reduced proportionally, and the entire
[p047] surface should be wiped. Table 9 lists the maximum allowable surface contamination for
[p047] different radionuclides in a restricted area.
[p047] Table 9. Surface contamination levels in restricted areas

[p048] 7.2.4 Medical/Nonmedical emergencies (e.g., “dirty bomb”)
[p048] Primary Response:
[p048] Always address life- or limb-threatening medical situations first and foremost.
[p048] Background
[p048] A "dirty bomb" is a type of "radiological dispersal device" (RDD) that combines a
[p048] conventional explosive, such as dynamite, with radioactive material. The terms dirty bomb
[p048] and RDD are often used interchangeably. Most RDDs would not release enough radiation to
[p048] kill people or cause severe illness – the conventional explosive itself would be more harmful
[p048] to people than the radioactive material. However, an RDD explosion could create fear and
[p048] panic, contaminate property, and require potentially costly cleanup.
[p048] A dirty bomb is not a nuclear bomb. A nuclear bomb creates an explosion that is millions of
[p048] times more powerful than a dirty bomb. The cloud of radiation from a nuclear bomb could
[p048] spread thousands of square miles, whereas a dirty bomb’s radiation could be dispersed within
[p048] a few blocks or miles of the explosion. A dirty bomb is not a "weapon of mass destruction"
[p048] but a "weapon of mass disruption," where contamination and anxiety are the major
[p048] objectives.
[p048] Impact of a Dirty Bomb
[p048] The extent of local contamination would depend on a number of factors, including the size of
[p048] the explosive, the amount and type of radioactive material used, the means of dispersal, and
[p048] weather conditions. Those closest to the RDD would be most likely to be injured by the
[p048] explosion. As radioactive material spreads, it becomes less concentrated and less harmful.
[p048] Prompt detection of the type of radioactive material used will greatly assist local authorities
[p048] in advising the community on protective measures, such as sheltering in place or quickly
[p048] leaving the immediate area. Radiation can be readily detected with equipment already carried
[p048] by many emergency responders. Subsequent decontamination of the affected area may
[p048] involve considerable time and expense.
[p048] Immediate health effects from exposure to the low radiation levels expected from an RDD
[p048] would likely be minimal. The effects of radiation exposure would be determined by:
[p048] • The amount of radiation absorbed by the body.
[p048] • The type of radiation (gamma, beta, or alpha).
[p048] • The distance from the source of radiation to an individual.
[p048] • The means of exposure – external or internal (absorbed by the skin, inhaled, or
[p048] ingested).
[p048] • The length of time exposed.
[p048] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 48
[p049] The health effects of radiation tend to be directly proportional to radiation dose. The higher
[p049] the radiation dose, the higher the risk and severity of injury.
[p049] Protective Actions
[p049] In general, protection from radiation is afforded by:
[p049] • Minimizing the time exposed to radioactive materials.
[p049] • Maximizing the distance from the source of radiation.
[p049] • Shielding from external exposure and inhaling radioactive material.
[p049] Control of Radioactive Material
[p049] Radioactive materials are routinely used at hospitals, research facilities, and industrial and
[p049] construction sites. These radioactive materials are used for such purposes as diagnosing and
[p049] treating illnesses, sterilizing equipment, and inspecting welding seams. The NRC and its
[p049] Agreement States, which also regulate radioactive material, administer more than 22,000
[p049] licenses of such materials. The majority of these materials would not be useful in an RDD.
[p049] The NRC and its Agreement States have in place a multi-layered, comprehensive security
[p049] program to protect these sources. This program has been effective, keeping incidents to a
[p049] minimum and their consequences low. Most lost or stolen sources are quickly found, with
[p049] little or no radiation exposure or contamination. The NRC continues to work at home and
[p049] abroad to make risk-significant radiation sources even more secure. The United States was
[p049] the first country to require enhanced security measures for radioactive sources, and the NRC
[p049] continues to lead the world in source security.
[p049] Other Contact Information
[p049] A number of federal agencies have responsibilities for dealing with RDDs, including the
[p049] Department of Homeland Security, Environmental Protection Agency, and Federal
[p049] Emergency Management Agency. Their public affairs offices can answer questions on the
[p049] subject or provide access to experts in and out of government.
[p049] Accidents Involving Personal Injury
[p049] For any accident involving personal injury, medical treatment or assistance will always be the
[p049] first priority. This may involve administering first aid and/or calling 911 for emergency
[p049] medical assistance. For accidents involving radioactive materials, contamination control and
[p049] exposure control are important but should never delay or impede medical assistance. If
[p049] radioactive materials are involved, emergency personnel should be notified before treatment
[p049] takes place, so they can take appropriate action to protect themselves as well as prevent the
[p049] spread of contamination. After the injured person is treated and removed from the accident
[p049] site, the previously described procedures should be followed as appropriate.
[p049] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 49
[p050] Personal Contamination
[p050] In the event of any personal contamination, laboratory personnel should follow these steps:
[p050] 1. Remove all contaminated laboratory personal protective clothing (lab coat, gloves,
[p050] 2. Wash the contaminated area with mild soap and water if possible.
[p050] 3. Monitor the contaminated area; repeat washing as necessary.
[p050] Decontamination Procedures
[p050] If surfaces or equipment within the laboratory are suspected or determined to be
[p050] contaminated with radioactive material, the radionuclide user must initiate and complete
[p050] appropriate decontamination procedures. For most relatively minor contamination incidents,
[p050] the following general steps should be taken upon discovery of the contamination:
[p050] 1. Mark the perimeter of the contaminated area.
[p050] 2. Notify the RSO of the contamination so that their staff can more accurately assess
[p050] the extent of the contamination and advise and assist in the decontamination effort.
[p050] 3. Assemble cleaning supplies (paper towels, detergent in water, plastic bags, and
[p050] plastic gloves); proceed with scrubbing the area from the borders to the center,
[p050] cleaning small areas at a time.
[p050] 4. Periodically monitor the effectiveness of the decontamination effort with surface
[p050] wipes and portable instrument surveys; place all contaminated cleaning materials,
[p050] including paper towels, rags, and gloves, in a plastic bag and label as "radioactive
[p050] waste."
[p050] 5. Notify Environmental Health and Safety upon completion of the decontamination
[p050] effort so that a follow-up contamination survey can be made.
[p050] Acute Radiation Syndrome
[p050] Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS) is defined as radiation exposures that involve the
[p050] following:
[p050] • Acute high dose (>0.7 Gy) radiation exposure.
[p050] • High dose rate in a short time interval.
[p050] • External dose of penetrating radiation.
[p050] • Greater than 70% of the body is exposed to the radiation (whole body exposure).
[p050] Patients with ARS develop classic signs and symptoms that run a typical time course,
[p050] regardless of the exposure dose. Patients undergo four phases during that time course:
[p050] prodromal phase, latent phase, manifest illness, and recovery or death. With increasing doses,
[p050] the time interval for each phase is shortened and the symptoms worsen. The signs, symptoms,
[p050] and considerations for each phase are found in Figure 5.
[p050] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 50
[p051] Figure 5. The phases of acute radiation syndrome
[p051] In the manifest illness phase of ARS, patients will experience symptoms consistent with the
[p051] dose of radiation they were exposed to. These symptoms can be grouped into subsyndromes
[p051] of ARS that correspond with the organ system or cell type that has been critically injured.
[p051] They are hematopoietic subsyndrome (bone marrow toxicity), gastrointestinal subsyndrome
[p051] (gastrointestinal stem cell toxicity), and neurovascular subsyndrome (neurovascular
[p051] endothelial cell toxicity). Figure 6 describes these subsyndromes in more detail, including the
[p051] doses at which they manifest and patient symptoms.
[p051] Figure 6. Acute radiation subsyndromes
[p051] The estimated LD of acute, whole-body, low-LET irradiation is 3.5 to 4.0 Gy without
[p051] medical intervention. However, swift medical intervention can increase the likelihood of
[p051] survival. Administering antibiotics and supportive treatment can increase the LD to 4.5 to
[p051] 7 Gy, and intensive care, reverse isolation, and hematopoietic cell transplantation can
[p051] potentially increase it to 7 to 9 Gy.
[p051] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 51
[p052] FDA-approved treatments generally target hematopoietic subsyndrome effects; however, new
[p052] treatments are looking at gastrointestinal subsyndrome as well. The following colony
[p052] stimulating factors (CSF) have been approved for ARS treatment:
[p052] • Filgramostim (NeupogenR)
[p052] • PEGylated filgramostim (NeulastaR)
[p052] • Sargramostim (LeukineR)
[p052] These agents predominantly promote neutrophil recovery, while sargramostim also promotes
[p052] additional bone marrow cell lineages. The FDA has also approved a thrombopoietin (TPO)
[p052] receptor activator that promotes megakaryocytes and platelets for ARS treatment:
[p052] romiplostim (NplateR).
[p052] A logical thought is that stem cell transplant would be an appropriate treatment for
[p052] hematopoietic subsyndrome. Unfortunately, historical data on the use of stem cell transplants
[p052] is limited and mixed with a very low and questionable success rate.
[p052] In addition to these targeted therapies, treatment of ARS depends on careful management of
[p052] patients susceptible to infection, bleeding, nutritional deficiencies, and electrolyte
[p052] abnormalities. Consequently, treatment often involves IV fluids/electrolytes, blood products
[p052] (irradiated and leukocyte-reduced to prevent infection and graft versus host disease), and
[p052] antimicrobial prophylaxis.
[p052] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 52
[p053] Appendix 1. Radiation-Measuring Instrumentation and Quality Control Tests
[p053] A Geiger-Mueller Counter has two main parts — a sealed tube, or chamber, filled with gas
[p053] and an information display. Radiation enters the tube and when it collides with the gas, it
[p053] pushes an electron away from the gas atom and creates an ion pair. A wire in the middle of
[p053] the tube attracts electrons, creating other ion pairs and sending a current through the wire.
[p053] The current goes to the information display and moves a needle across a scale or makes a
[p053] number display on a screen. These devices usually provide "counts per minute," or the
[p053] number of ion pairs created every 60 seconds. A Geiger counter indicates when an ion pair is
[p053] created, but nothing about the type of radiation or its energy.
[p053] Well Counters are used for high-sensitivity counting of radioactive specimens such as blood
[p053] or urine samples or “wipes” from surveys of removable contamination (i.e., wipe testing). A
[p053] well counter includes a cylindric scintillation crystal (most commonly thallium-doped sodium
[p053] iodide) with a circular bore (well) for the sample, backed by a photomultiplier tube (PMT)
[p053] and its associated electronics. Modern scintillation well counters are often equipped with a
[p053] multichannel analyzer (MCA) for energy (i.e., isotope)-selective counting and an automatic
[p053] sample changer for unattended counting of multiple samples. Because of their high intrinsic
[p053] and geometric efficiencies (resulting from the use of a thick crystal and a well-type counting
[p053] geometry, respectively), well counters are extremely sensitive and, in fact, can reliably be
[p053] used only for counting activities up to approximately 37 kBq (0.001 mCi); at higher
[p053] activities, dead-time counting losses become prohibitive and the measured counts, inaccurate.
[p053] Scintillation Detection Systems use a substance that creates a flash of light when ionizing
[p053] radiation interacts with it. Liquid scintillation counting is an analytical technique used to
[p053] identify and quantify low levels of radioactivity, such as for contamination or for particulate
[p053] emitting (b and a) radioactive samples. Solid scintillators are sensitive to gamma radiation
[p053] and can measure dose rates in the μrem/hr range.
[p053] An Ionization Chamber is an instrument used to measure the number of ions within a
[p053] medium. It usually consists of a gas-filled enclosure between two conducting electrodes (the
[p053] anode and cathode). When gas between the electrodes is ionized by any means, such as by
[p053] gamma rays or other radioactive emission, the ions and dissociated electrons move to the
[p053] electrodes of the opposite polarity, thus creating an ionization current that may be measured.
[p053] Each ion essentially deposits or removes a small electric charge to or from an electrode, such
[p053] that the accumulated charge is proportional to the number of like-charged ions. Ionization
[p053] chambers can be open or sealed depending on their function. Open ionization chambers are
[p053] typically used as personal dosimeters. Radioisotope dose calibrators are sealed ionization
[p053] chambers widely used in the nuclear industry as they provide an output that is proportional to
[p053] radiation dose and are calibrated to specific isotopes.
[p053] Radio-thin-layer Chromatography (radio-TLC) is commonly used to analyze purity of
[p053] radiopharmaceuticals or to determine the reaction conversion when optimizing radiosynthesis
[p053] processes. In applications where there are few radioactive species, radio-TLC is preferred
[p053] over radio-high-performance liquid chromatography due to its simplicity and relatively quick
[p053] analysis time. Most approaches to radio-TLC were merely extensions or modifications of
[p053] techniques that had been used in paper chromatography (PC).
[p053] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 53
